feed_card.headline: Per unit of energy, nuclear power kills roughly 800 times fewer people than coal, even counting Chernobyl and Fukushima.
teasers: The safest power source is also the scariest
The deaths that arrive without a headline
Why Fukushima's radiation killed so few

sections.content: Per unit of energy, nuclear power kills roughly 800 times fewer people than coal, even counting Chernobyl and Fukushima.

sections.content: By the numbers that count deaths, nuclear power sits with wind and solar, among the safest sources there are. That is the reverse of how it is usually ranked. The deaths come from air. The exhaust of burning coal, oil, and gas kills millions each year, while the fear stays fixed on a few dramatic accidents.

content.title: Air pollution does most of the killing
content.body: The death rate folds in two very different kinds of harm, both counted in the same figure. One is a steady background hum, the other a spike every few decades. For fossil fuels, it is mostly the air. Fine soot and gases from combustion settle in lungs and arteries, quietly shortening lives across the world, year after year. For nuclear, it is mostly the rare, catastrophic accident.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 92 400 116' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='118' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>Coal's toll, by cause</text><rect x='40' y='140' width='294' height='28' rx='8' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.18'/><rect x='340' y='140' width='34' height='28' rx='8' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.5'/><text x='187' y='190' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>air pollution</text><text x='357' y='190' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' opacity='0.7' text-anchor='middle'>accidents</text></svg>

content.title: The accidents everyone fears, in scale
content.body: Two accidents drive the fear, and both are already folded into nuclear's 0.03. Count them plainly. Chernobyl killed about 30 workers and firefighters within weeks in 1986, and its long-term cancer estimates run from a few hundred to roughly 4,000. At Fukushima in 2011, one death has been tied to radiation. The evacuation that followed, not the radiation, caused about 2,300 more.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><text x='200' y='26' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='14' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75' text-anchor='middle'>The two accidents, counted plainly</text><text x='24' y='72' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='start'>Chernobyl</text><rect x='110' y='58' width='250' height='20' rx='6' fill='var(--accent)' fill-opacity='0.18' stroke='var(--accent)' stroke-width='2.5'/><text x='118' y='72' fill='var(--accent)' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' font-weight='700' text-anchor='start'>up to 4,000</text><text x='110' y='96' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='start'>about 30 acute, the rest long-term estimates</text><text x='24' y='146' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='13' font-weight='600' text-anchor='start'>Fukushima</text><rect x='110' y='132' width='144' height='20' rx='6' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' opacity='0.55'/><text x='118' y='146' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-mono), ui-monospace, monospace' font-size='13' font-weight='700' opacity='0.8' text-anchor='start'>~2,300</text><text x='110' y='170' fill='currentColor' font-family='var(--font-sans), system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='12' opacity='0.6' text-anchor='start'>nearly all from the evacuation, not radiation</text></svg>

sections.content: We judge danger mostly by how vivid it is. A meltdown has a date and a place; air pollution has neither, so its far larger toll barely registers. On the narrow question of deaths per unit of energy, the answer has been clear for years. What we choose to build still turns on cost, waste, and where the sun and wind are strong.

content.question: Measured by deaths per unit of energy, how does nuclear power compare with coal?
options: Nuclear is slightly more dangerous
They come out about equal
Nuclear causes far fewer deaths, on the order of 800 times fewer
Coal is safer once accidents are counted
content.explanation: Coal runs about 24 deaths per terawatt-hour and nuclear about 0.03, a gap near 800 to one. Wind and solar sit close to nuclear.

content.question: For coal, oil, and gas, what accounts for most of the deaths in these figures?
options: Explosions at the power plants
Chronic air pollution from burning them
Radiation leaks
Dam failures
content.explanation: Most fossil-fuel deaths come from breathing combustion pollution over years, not from accidents. That toll is large but diffuse, so it rarely makes the news.

content.question: What caused most of the deaths connected to the Fukushima accident?
options: Direct radiation exposure
The reactor explosion itself
Contaminated food
The stress and disruption of the mass evacuation
content.explanation: Only one death there has been officially tied to radiation. The roughly 2,300 other deaths came from the forced evacuation, not from the radiation.

content.question: Why do people tend to rate nuclear as more dangerous than the death data suggest?
options: Its deaths arrive in rare, vivid accidents, while fossil deaths are slow and spread out
Nuclear plants are much newer than coal plants
The underlying data are usually wrong
Coal's death toll is deliberately exaggerated
content.explanation: A single accident is a dramatic, datable event, while air-pollution deaths are quiet and diffuse. The mind weighs the vivid risk far more heavily.

content.question: Does deaths per unit of energy settle the entire case for nuclear power?
options: Yes, it answers every question about it
No, it leaves out waste, cost, and proliferation
Yes, because it already counts accidents
No, because it ignores air pollution
content.explanation: The metric captures deaths well, but building choices also turn on waste storage, cost, proliferation risk, and land, which it does not measure.

content.question: The nuclear figure of about 0.03 deaths per unit of energy includes what?
options: Only routine operation, with no accidents
Only the Fukushima accident
Chernobyl and Fukushima, folded into the total
Deaths from every kind of power plant
content.explanation: The worst nuclear accidents are already counted in that number, and nuclear still lands among the safest sources by this measure.

content.myth: Nuclear power is the most dangerous way to make electricity.
content.reality: By deaths per unit of energy, it is one of the safest, close to wind and solar. Fossil fuels kill far more, mostly through air pollution.

content.myth: Fukushima's radiation killed thousands of people.
content.reality: One worker's death has been officially attributed to radiation. The more than 2,000 other deaths came from the stress and disruption of the evacuation, not from the radiation itself.

content.myth: So the numbers are settled and nuclear is beyond debate.
content.reality: Not entirely. Chernobyl's long-term cancer toll is still uncertain, with estimates ranging widely. And deaths per unit of energy leave out waste, cost, and proliferation, which are real parts of the argument.

content.body: The hardest number to pin down is Chernobyl's long-term toll. Estimating deaths from low doses of radiation spread across decades and a whole continent is genuinely difficult, and honest experts differ by thousands. Whether very low levels of radiation carry risk in proportion to the dose, or almost none at all, is itself unsettled.
items: How many cancers did Chernobyl's fallout cause across Europe?
Does very low-dose radiation harm in proportion to dose, or barely at all?
